title: CRS 001 — LCEL Code Workflow
subtitle: Templates, invoke rules, pipes, parsers, and provider switching

nodes.label: PromptTemplate
nodes.definition: Reusable string template with variables — filled by prompt.invoke(), not by the LLM.
nodes.whyItMatters: First lab pattern; every chain starts here.
nodes.safeSentence: PromptTemplate.from_template creates a reusable prompt; invoke fills variables.
nodes.commonTrap: Importing PromptTemplate from the wrong module or skipping .from_template().
nodes.interviewAnswer: I use PromptTemplate.from_template for single-string prompts and invoke with a dict of variables.

note.summary:
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate

prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Explain {topic}.")
formatted = prompt.invoke({"topic": "RAG"})
print(formatted.to_string())

nodes.label: ChatPromptTemplate
nodes.definition: Role-based messages — system, human, assistant tuples via from_messages().
nodes.whyItMatters: Chat models expect message lists, not one flat string.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Chat prompts use message roles; from_messages is the usual constructor.

note.summary:
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

chat = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful tutor."),
    ("human", "Explain {topic}.")
])

nodes.label: Few-Shot Template
nodes.definition: Embeds example input/output pairs in the prompt to steer model behavior.
nodes.safeSentence: Few-shot adds examples so the model sees the pattern you want.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Few-shot prompting supplies examples in the template when zero-shot is inconsistent.

note.summary:
FewShotPromptTemplate combines example_selector + suffix/prefix.
See lab 04 for the course-local Runnable-style few-shot pattern.

nodes.label: prompt.invoke()
nodes.definition: Runs the prompt component only — fills variables and builds a prompt value.
nodes.safeSentence: prompt.invoke() prepares input; it does not call the LLM.
nodes.commonTrap: Expecting a model answer from prompt.invoke().
nodes.interviewAnswer: prompt.invoke prepares templated input; model.invoke generates; chain.invoke runs the full pipeline.
relatedQuestions: What does prompt.invoke do vs model.invoke?
When do I still not need an API key?

nodes.label: chain.invoke()
nodes.definition: Runs every runnable connected in the LCEL chain end to end.
nodes.whyItMatters: The main execution entry point for composed pipelines.
nodes.safeSentence: chain.invoke() runs prompt, model, parser, and any other linked steps.
nodes.commonTrap: Manually calling each step when one chain.invoke() would do.
nodes.interviewAnswer: chain.invoke executes the full LCEL pipeline with one call and consistent input shape.

nodes.label: LCEL Pipe
nodes.definition: Compose runnables with the pipe operator: prompt | model | parser.
nodes.whyItMatters: Core Module 2 code pattern validated in local labs.
nodes.safeSentence: The pipe builds a chain; invoke once at the end.
nodes.interviewAnswer: LCEL uses | to connect runnables into a reusable chain with a single invoke interface.

note.summary:
chain = prompt | model | parser
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "RAG"})

Local lab 05 uses a stand-in model function before provider-backed 05b/05c.

nodes.label: RunnableParallel
nodes.definition: Run multiple branches from one input dict and merge named outputs.
nodes.whyItMatters: Useful when one user question needs multiple sub-answers.
nodes.safeSentence: RunnableParallel fans out work; each branch is a named key in the result.
nodes.interviewAnswer: RunnableParallel executes branches concurrently and returns a dict of branch outputs.

note.summary:
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel

parallel = RunnableParallel(summary=chain_a, keywords=chain_b)
parallel.invoke({"text": doc})

nodes.label: JsonOutputParser
nodes.definition: Parses model text into JSON-shaped Python data at the end of a chain.
nodes.whyItMatters: Bridge from free-form LLM text to application data structures.
nodes.safeSentence: Parser is the last pipe segment when you need JSON fields.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I append JsonOutputParser when the app needs structured fields from model text.

note.summary:
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser

chain = prompt | model | JsonOutputParser()
# Model should return JSON text the parser can load.

nodes.label: Structured Output
nodes.definition: Provider-native or Pydantic-validated objects instead of manual JSON parsing.
nodes.whyItMatters: Safer contracts for downstream Python code.
nodes.safeSentence: Prefer structured output when the provider supports schema-bound responses.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I use provider-native structured output or Pydantic parsers when field types must be reliable.

note.summary:
See lab 09 for provider-native structured output with a Pydantic model.
Pattern: model.with_structured_output(MySchema) or parser bound to schema.

nodes.label: Provider Switch
nodes.definition: Same chain shape, different model adapter — OpenAI vs watsonx.
nodes.whyItMatters: Shows LCEL reusability beyond one vendor.
nodes.safeSentence: Swap the model runnable; keep prompt and parser stable.
nodes.interviewAnswer: LCEL lets me swap provider adapters while preserving prompt and parser structure.

note.summary:
Labs 05b (OpenAI), 05c/05d (watsonx), 05f (switching) share the same chain skeleton.
Only the model runnable and env vars change.

nodes.label: Gen AI Landscape
nodes.definition: Return to the big-picture map — models, prompts, LangChain, delivery.
nodes.whyItMatters: Keeps code workflow connected to course concepts.
nodes.safeSentence: Open the landscape map when you need why before how.
nodes.commonTrap: Memorizing syntax without the stack context.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I connect LCEL patterns back to the broader generative application stack.

paths.label: Lab Lane
paths.description: Match local lab order: templates → LCEL pipe → invoke.

paths.label: Parser Lane
paths.description: From pipe to JSON and structured output.

paths.label: Memory Lane
paths.description: Invoke rules and traps from RemNote reinforcement.
